name: Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop
name_original: Parque Natural de Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche – circular por el Camino de Santa Ana a Alájar
description: This difficult 9.2-mile (14.9 km) jogging loop in Sierra de Aracena offers varied terrain, historical sites, and village views.
region_intro: Jogging around Alájar provides diverse routes through the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park, characterized by undulating hills and extensive forests. The region features varied terrain, from gentle slopes to more challenging ascents, with woodlands of cork oaks and holm oaks offering ample shade. This landscape includes dramatic rocky outcrops like Peña Arias Montano, providing panoramic views, and "dehesas" with their unique ecological interest. Best jogging routes around Alájar The most popular jogging route is Pico La Era Loop, a 7.3 miles (11.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 35 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the varied terrain of the natural park. Another top favourite among local runners is Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop, a difficult 9.2 miles (14.9 km) path. This route connects two villages, offering a mix of cultural sights and natural landscapes. Local runners also love the Running loop from Linares de la Sierra, a 7.0 miles (11.3 km) trail leading through Mediterranean woodlands and rolling hills, often completed in about 1 hour 27 minutes. Jogging around Alájar is defined by varied terrain, extensive forests, and picturesque village-to-village connections. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 200 runners have used komoot to explore Alájar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running trails are available around Alájar?
A: There are over 60 running routes around Alájar, offering a wide variety of options for different fitness levels. These include 23 moderate trails and 43 more challenging routes, ensuring there's something for every runner.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging routes near Alájar?
A: The running routes around Alájar feature a diverse and picturesque terrain. You'll encounter rolling hills, challenging ascents, and extensive forests dominated by cork oaks, holm oaks, and chestnut trees. Many paths also traverse "dehesas," semi-open woodlands with grasslands, providing varied scenery and ecological interest.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier run?
A: Yes, while many routes offer a challenge, there are 23 moderate running trails around Alájar. These paths provide a good workout without extreme difficulty, often winding through picturesque villages and shaded woodlands. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20121846" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Ana to Alájar Trail – Santa Ana la Real loop from Santa Ana la Real&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option.

Q: What is the best time of year for trail running in Alájar?
A: The extensive forests around Alájar provide ample shade, making it possible to run comfortably even during warmer periods. However, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for trail running, with lush landscapes and vibrant colors. Early mornings are also recommended during summer months to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Can I find running routes with panoramic viewpoints in Alájar?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its stunning vistas. A prominent natural landmark is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4184233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Arias Montano&lt;/a&gt;, which offers breathtaking panoramic views of Alájar, its olive groves, and the surrounding undulating plains. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31452411" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Marines Village – Alájar Viewpoint loop from Los Marines&lt;/a&gt; specifically incorporate viewpoints into the experience.

Q: Are there any natural landmarks or attractions to see along the running paths?
A: Yes, the trails often pass by significant natural and historical sites. You can encounter the dramatic rocky outcrop of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4184233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Arias Montano&lt;/a&gt; and its historic hermitage, or even a &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7287170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;waterfall in the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;. The region's "dehesas" also offer unique ecological interest.

Q: Are there circular running routes available?
A: Yes, many of the running routes around Alájar are designed as loops, allowing you to experience varied scenery without repetition. Popular circular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984466988/pico-la-era-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico La Era Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984506054/alajar-and-santa-ana-la-real-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which connects two charming villages.

Q: Are the running trails in Alájar suitable for families?
A: While many routes involve varied terrain and elevation, there are moderate trails that families might enjoy. It's advisable to check the difficulty and length of specific routes to ensure they match your family's fitness level. The shaded forests can make for a pleasant experience, especially on shorter, less strenuous paths.

Q: Are there dog-friendly running routes in the Alájar area?
A: As Alájar is located within the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park, dogs are generally welcome on trails but should be kept on a leash to protect local wildlife and livestock. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you carry waste bags to maintain the natural beauty of the park.

Q: Where can I find parking near the running trails in Alájar?
A: Parking is typically available in and around the villages that serve as starting points for many routes, such as Alájar itself or nearby Linares de la Sierra. Look for designated parking areas within these settlements, which often provide convenient access to the trailheads.

Q: Are there places to eat or drink near the running trails?
A: Yes, many running routes connect or pass through charming villages like Alájar, Linares de la Sierra, or Santa Ana la Real. These villages offer local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can refuel and enjoy traditional Andalusian cuisine after your run.

Q: What do other runners enjoy the most about jogging in Alájar?
A: The komoot community highly rates the running experience in Alájar, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Runners often praise the diverse terrain, the extensive and shaded forests, and the picturesque village-to-village connections that offer a mix of cultural sights and natural landscapes.
tips: The Camino de Santa Ana to Alájar is a popular hiking route in the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche, in the province of Huelva. This trail connects the towns of Santa Ana la Real and Alájar, offering a picturesque route through natural landscapes and points of historical interest.😊

The Hermitage of the Queen of the Angels is a Catholic temple located in the Peña de Arias Montano, belonging to the town of Alájar. Every September 8, it hosts the pilgrimage of the Queen of the Angels, the one that summons the most pilgrims in the Sierra de Aracena region.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop for jogging?
faqs.answer: This particular loop, at nearly 15 km with over 430 meters of elevation gain, is rated as difficult for jogging. It requires good fitness and sure-footedness due to varied terrain and ascents. While some shorter variations exist that are easier, this specific route is best suited for experienced trail runners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, featuring predominantly unpaved paths, hiking trails, and tracks. You should expect sections with loose stones, cobbled tracks, and some paved areas. The route also involves crossing several streams and a river, which can be muddy or humid, especially after rain. You'll traverse wooded pastures (dehesa) with cork oaks, and encounter short, steep inclines and descents.

faqs.question: What notable sights or landmarks will I see on the route?
faqs.answer: The loop offers beautiful scenery and historical elements. You'll pass through picturesque cork oak forests and traditional white villages like Alájar and Santa Ana la Real. Notable highlights include the Santa Ana to Alájar Trail and the Arias Montano Peak and the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Angels. You might also spot preserved lime kilns, old mills, and the unique 'Bosque de las Letras' (Forest of Letters) near Santa Ana la Real.

faqs.question: Is the Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, as it passes through the Parque Natural Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche, it's essential to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and livestock, especially in areas with Iberian pigs. Always carry water for your dog and clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this loop?
faqs.answer: Spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are generally the best times. During these seasons, the weather is milder, and the natural landscape is at its most vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making jogging challenging, while winters can be damp and cool, with potential for muddy sections after rain.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to jog the Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop, as it is a public trail within the natural park. However, always respect private property and follow local regulations.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the villages of Alájar or Santa Ana la Real, which serve as access points for the loop. Specific parking areas might be available near the village centers or designated trailheads. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: What should I know about jogging in the Parque Natural Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Parque Natural Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche, an area known for its unique dehesa landscape and rich biodiversity. While there are no entrance fees, visitors are encouraged to respect the natural environment, stay on marked paths, avoid littering, and be mindful of local wildlife such as Iberian pigs, otters, and various bird species. The air is often scented with wild thyme, rosemary, and pine.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other official trails. You'll find sections that are part of the Tierra de los Descubrimientos, Alajar, La Peña y sus aldeas, Risco Levante-Los Chorros, and the Camino de Santa Ana a Alajar, among others. This offers opportunities to explore different paths if you wish to extend or modify your run.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter along the way?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Aracena is rich in wildlife. You might spot various bird species, including black storks and vultures. Mammals like otters and genets inhabit the area, and the famous Iberian pigs graze freely in the dehesa. Keep an eye out for these animals, but always observe them from a respectful distance.

faqs.question: Are there any easier or shorter variations of this loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, while the full Alájar and Santa Ana la Real Loop is challenging, there are shorter, more moderate options available in the area. For example, some routes are around 6-7 km with less elevation gain, classified as 'moderate' or even 'easy,' suitable for a more relaxed jogging experience. These variations often focus on specific sections like the 'Santa Ana to Alájar Trail – Dehesa del Ibérico loop'.

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for jogging this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the varied and sometimes challenging terrain, it's crucial to wear appropriate trail running shoes with good grip and ankle support. Bring sufficient water, especially on warmer days, as water sources might be limited. A map or GPS device (like komoot's app) is recommended, along with sun protection, snacks, and a basic first-aid kit.

waypoints.name: Arias Montano Peak and the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Angels
